text:
Admission Date:  2008-01-31       Discharge Date:  1977-04-11

Date of Birth:   2003-06-02       Sex:  M

Service:

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  This is a 61-year-old male
patient with new onset of chest pain with a previous known
history of coronary artery disease.

In 26 of this year, the patient had chest pain as well
as dyspnea on exertion and was admitted to Chesapeake Regional Medical Center.  He ruled in for a myocardial infarction and inferior
myocardial infarction and was taken to Cardiac
Catheterization Laboratory.  At that time, he had an
angioplasty of the circumflex artery.  The catheter also
showed significant 3-vessel coronary artery disease as well
as a left ventricular ejection fraction of 50%.

The patient has subsequently been referred for coronary
artery bypass graft.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1.  Seizure disorder diagnosed 12 years ago; he has not had
any seizures since then and has been maintained on Dilantin.
2.  He also has had a recent urinary tract infection but has
completed his antibiotic course.
3.  He also has a history of hypertension.
4.  History of hypercholesterolemia.

MEDICATIONS ON ADMISSION:  Preoperative medications included
lisinopril, Toprol, Zocor, Plavix, aspirin, Dilantin 400 mg
by mouth once per day, and ciprofloxacin (which is the
antibiotic - he completed the course for a preoperative
urinary tract infection).

ALLERGIES:  The patient states no known drug allergies.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  A former smoker; quit five months ago.  He
is married and lives with his wife.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION ON PRESENTATION:  Physical examination
preoperatively was unremarkable.

BRIEF SUMMARY OF HOSPITAL COURSE:   The patient was taken to
the operating room on 2008-01-31 where he underwent
coronary artery bypass graft times four with a left internal
mammary artery to the left anterior descending artery,
saphenous vein graft to the posterior descending artery, and
a sequential saphenous vein graft to the obtuse marginal and
to the right intermedius.

Postoperatively, he was transferred from the operating room
to the Coronary Care Unit on a Neo-Synephrine, nitroglycerin,
and propofol drips in stable condition.  He was also atrially
paced with via his epicardial pacemaker wires.

On postoperative day one, the patient had been weaned from
mechanical ventilation and successfully extubated and was
hemodynamically stable.  He had converted to his normal sinus
rhythm with a blood pressure of 108/46.

On postoperative day one, the patient's chest tubes were
discontinued and he was hemodynamically stable and ready to
be transferred to the telemetry floor; however, there were no
beds available at that time, and he remained in the Intensive
Care Unit.  He was begun on his oral medications.  The
patient was beginning to get out bed and progress with
cardiac rehabilitation and physical therapy.

The patient was ultimately transferred out of the Intensive
Care Unit on postoperative day two.  He continued to progress
with cardiac rehabilitation and remained in a normal sinus
rhythm.  His epicardial pacemaker wires were discontinued on
postoperative day three.  His oral medications, beta
blockers, were increased.  The patient continued to progress
from a physical therapy standpoint.

The patient progressed well over the next few days.  His
Imdur was discontinued.  He was placed on Imdur
postoperatively because of significant spasm in the operating
room of his left internal mammary artery graft.  This was
discontinued on postoperative day six due to hypotension.
Otherwise, he remained hemodynamically stable throughout his
course.  His Foley catheter was discontinued and he was
urinating without difficulty and remained stable and was
ready to be discharged home today; on postoperative day
seven.

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:  The patient was afebrile with a
temperature of 97.4.  His blood pressure was 98/56.  He was
in a normal sinus rhythm with a rate of 80s.  His respiratory
rate was 18.  His oxygen saturation was 97% on room air.  His
weight today was 52.5 kilograms (which was slightly below his
preoperative weight of 122 pounds).  Neurologically, the
patient was intact.  His cardiac examination was regular rate
and rhythm.  His lungs were clear to auscultation
bilaterally.  His abdomen was soft, nontender, and
nondistended with positive bowel sounds.  His extremities had
no edema.  His sternal wound was clean, dry, and intact with
Steri-Strips in place.  His lower leg incision was also
clean, dry, and intact with some old ecchymosis noted in the
area.  There was no erythema.

MEDICATIONS ON DISCHARGE:  (Discharge medications were as
follows)
1.  Colace 100 mg by mouth twice per day.
2.  Ranitidine 150 mg by mouth twice per day.
3.  Enteric-coated aspirin 325 mg by mouth every day.
4.  Dilantin 400 mg by mouth once per day.
5.  Dilaudid 2 mg by mouth q.4-6h. as needed (for pain).
6.  Lopressor 25 mg by mouth twice per day.
7.  Zocor 10 mg by mouth once per day.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS/FOLLOWUP:
1.  The patient was instructed to follow up with his primary
care physician (Dr. Logan Foster) in one to two weeks.
2.  The patient was instructed to follow up with his
cardiologist (Dr. Amanda Parker) in one to two weeks.
3.  The patient was instructed to follow up with Dr. Cheryl
[**Last Name (Prefixes) **] from Cardiothoracic Surgery in three to four
weeks.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:
1.  Coronary artery disease.
2.  Status post coronary artery bypass graft.

CONDITION AT DISCHARGE:  Condition on discharge was good.





                            Donovan [**Last Name (Prefixes) 413**], M.D.  167

Dictated By:Heather Conway
MEDQUIST36
